title: Physics-constrained Real-time Hand Grasping in VR/AR via Force Closure Stability Judgment
authors: Junjian Lin, Yinhaoyu Jin, Jianjian Wang, Pingfa Feng, Dingwen Yu, Xiangyu Zhang, Jianfu Zhang
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Constructing hand-object interactions in digital environments holds significant value for robotic learning of object manipulation. We propose a physics-constrained real-time hand-object interaction method with two key innovations: (1) real-hand-driven articulation control under physical constraints, where the virtual hand follows real-hand motion through articulation joint drives while the physics engine handles collisions and surface sliding, enabling continuous gesture adjustment during contact; (2) force closure theory (FCT) for real-time stability judgment, enabling automatic grasp stability determination from contact points without prior object knowledge or training data. We implement and validate our approach on VR and AR headsets with visual-only hand tracking, demonstrating realistic hand-object interactions across various rigid object geometries in digital environments.

title: Towards Immersive Volumetric Space: Capture, Reconstruction, and Application
authors: Xiaohang Yu, Zhengxian Yang, Shi Pan, Yuqi Han, Haoxiang Wang, Jun Zhang, Shi Yan, Borong Lin, Lei Yang, Lu Fang, Tao Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Volumetric video is an emerging media format poised to support future technologies like holographic communication and VR/AR. However, current construction methods face challenges not only in algorithms and applications but also in data acquisition. Existing capture devices and methods struggle to provide expansive immersive volumetric space, with high-quality rendering limited to nearby capture paths or small captured volumes. In this paper, we propose a comprehensive solution encompassing data acquisition, reconstruction algorithm, and application. We introduce Den-SOFT, a dense space-oriented light field dataset featuring the densest distribution of viewpoints, captured using a mobile system with 40 GoPros@5K resolution. Each scene in Den-SOFT contains at least 1,000 images, achieving an average density (average view number within the captured volume) of 134.68/m3 along with various real-world effects such as infinite skies, reflections, and shadows. Recognizing that advanced reconstruction methods like 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) may yield limited quality results even with such dense data, we present Wild-GS for in-the-wild light field reconstruction. It enhances 3DGS by: i) introducing a Gaussian sky ball to address zero-disparity appearances and ii) employing explicit color mapping and gradient-guided weighted L1 loss to mitigate cross-view color inconsistencies. Wild-GS outperforms the latest 3DGS method, Scaffold-GS, for in-the-wild scenarios without compromising the explicit essence of the original 3DGS. Extensive experiments and live VR demonstrations validate the effectiveness of our solution from data capture to reconstruction and application.

title: Improving the Performance of Next-Generation Vehicular Safety Messages Communication With Relay-Assisted Protocol
authors: A. F. M. Shahen Shah, Muhammet Ali Karabulut, Haci Ilhan, Md Baharul Islam, Khaled M. Rabie, Mohammed Atiquzzaman
publish: IEEE Canadian Journal of Electrical and Computer Engineering
abstract: Vehicular ad hoc networks (VANETs) are becoming popular due to autonomy and intelligent transportation systems (ITSs), and they play a key role in enabling the broader concept of the Internet of Vehicles (IoV). Moreover, VANETs will have cutting-edge improvements with the emergence of 5G and 6G technologies. Recent research has concentrated on reducing delay and increasing throughput. To improve the performance of VANETs, a novel MIMO-OFDM-based cooperative vehicular safety message communication mechanism is proposed in this work. Optimal relay selection in cooperative communication is essential in maximizing system performance. This article presents an optimal relay selection mechanism based on the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and vehicle velocity for VANETs. First, an SNR-based relay selection mechanism is analyzed separately, and then an optimal relay selection mechanism is suggested based on vehicle velocity. To maximize the benefits of multiple-input–multiple-output (MIMO) and orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing (OFDM), both are combined, and a novel access mechanism is suggested to accommodate MIMO with OFDM. A Markov chain-based mathematical model is derived. Nakagami-m fading channels are considered in the analysis. The performance is evaluated in terms of successful transmission probability, outage probability, throughput, and delay terms. Simulation of urban mobility (SUMO) generates a microscopic mobility model for practical application. For the sake of comparison and to highlight the performance achievable gains, comparisons with existing mechanisms are presented, which reveal significant improvement in performance. The simulation results reveal that significant improvements can be obtained with the proposed system relative to other existing schemes.

title: Startup Success Predictor: A Machine Learning-Based Forecasting Framework for Early-Stage Venture Assessment
authors: Praveen M Naik, Samar Rihan, Shaun Marvell Rodrigues, Sushan S Shetty, Yash V Maurya
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: The venture capital landscape faces significant challenges in accurately predicting startup success, with failure rates exceeding 90% for early-stage companies. Traditional evaluation methods rely heavily on subjective assessments and domain expertise, often leading to inconsistent investment decisions. This paper presents a comprehensive machine learning-based forecasting framework designed to predict startup outcomes using quantitative metrics and historical performance data. We evaluated four distinct classification algorithms: Logistic Regression, Support Vector Machine (SVM), Random Forest, and Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) on a dataset comprising over 2,500 startup records. Our methodology incorporates extensive feature engineering, cross-validation techniques, and performance optimization strategies. The best-performing model, Gradient Boosting, achieved a test accuracy of 80% and ROC-AUC of 0.82, demonstrating significant potential for real-world deployment in venture capital decision-making processes. The framework addresses critical gaps in startup evaluation by providing data-driven insights that complement traditional due diligence methods.

title: Building a Multilingual, Voice-First Banking Assistant: Architecture and Implementation for the Mifos Ecosystem
authors: Akshat Sharma, Priyanshu Tiwari, Himanshu Tiwari, Rishabh Raj, Rahul Goel, Edward Cable, David Higgins
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: This paper introduces a voice-driven banking assistant leveraging Gemini and custom GPT-4o LLMs for natural language understanding, achieving 94.5% intent recognition accuracy and 91% end-to-end task success. The system integrates Whisper for speech-to-text and MMS for text-to-speech within a FastAPI backend, featuring voice authentication and a deepfake detection module with 96.2% security accuracy. Supporting multilingual financial operations with stateful conversations, this domain-specific AI agent demonstrates secure, reliable banking assistance through advanced LLM and voice authentication capabilities.

title: Learning External Point-Set Context for Point Cloud Segmentation
authors: Jibin Peng, Haotian Dong, Xin Wang, Di Lin, Haoran Chen, Ruonan Liu, Qin Chen, Yipeng Wu, Haoran Lu, Qing Guo, Yi Wang, Lingyu Liang, Wuyuan Xie, Miaohui Wang, Ping Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: Visual context is essential for point cloud semantic segmentation. The contextual information captures the semantic relationship between 3-D points, providing helpful hints for reasoning the category labels of points. Most current methods harness the internal context from the parts of the same object (or from the things within the same scene). In contrast, we propose the external point-set context (EPSC), allowing a richer context of external points learned across various objects/scenes to assist the segmentation task. We employ an external memory with multiple sets to store the EPSC representations learned from the training data. Each representation is a cluster feature, which captures the relationship between adjacent 3-D points for recognizing the semantic category of the center point. During the inference phase, the external memory releases the EPSC representations, providing rich and relevant context for segmenting the target point cloud. We extensively evaluate our method on Stanford Large-Scale 3-D Indoor Spaces (S3DIS), ScanNetv2, and ShapeNetPart datasets, where we achieve the result of effective improvement.

title: Indoor 5.8 GHz dc-dc Wireless Power Transfer at a Distance Capable of Attitude and Range Optimization
authors: Ricardo A. M. Pereira, Diogo Matos, Ricardo Figueiredo, Bernardo Mendes, Henrique F. Chaves, Helena Ribeiro, Helder Morais, Daniel Belo, J. M. Oliveira, Arnaldo Oliveira, Nuno Borges Carvalho
publish: IEEE Journal on Wireless Power Technologies
abstract: Wireless power transfer (WPT) promises to revolutionize the way we use electrical devices. When considering indoor applications, these devices may be moving inside a certain area while the transmitter is fixed, like an electrical appliance. In these scenarios, beam forming and beam steering capabilities are required for achieving high efficiency. By implementing the quasioptical approach, we can focus energy at specific positions on the radiative near-field region to reduce the spillover losses, which, when coupled to electronically configured phased arrays, allows coverage for moving targets in terms of attitude and range. Here, we present a system capable of doing that to transfer power wirelessly for indoor applications. It consists of a 4x4 phased array transmitter at 5.8 GHz, whose radiating elements are rectangular microstrip patch antennas. Dielectric lenses were implemented, to focus the energy and reduce the spillover losses. This paper presents the design procedure to achieve the system requirements, as well as experimental results of two system configurations, one with an optimized lens and another using two dielectric lenses, up to a maximum transfer distance of 2 m. Finally, an RF-dc converter was used, enabling dc-dc WPT, while a feedback channel closes the loop for automatic optimization.

title: A DRL-based Energy-Efficient and Mobility-aware SFC Embedding Strategy in 6G SAGINs
authors: Yi-Huai Hsu, Yu-Lun Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Networking
abstract: Space-air-ground-integrated networks (SAGINs) can meet the goal of creating a seamless global coverage for 6G. With software-defined networking and network function virtualization technologies, a service can be represented by a service function chain (SFC), which is a sequenced set of multiple virtual network functions (VNFs). Nevertheless, the embedding of VNFs of an SFC, referred to as the SFC embedding problem, including SFC placement and SFC migration, remarkably impacts the quality of experience (QoE) of user equipments (UEs) and the energy efficiency of an SAGIN. In this paper, we jointly investigate the SFC placement and migration in the SFC embedding problem in an SAGIN. We formulate this problem as a non-linear programming problem seeking to minimize SAGIN energy consumption while meeting the service downtime (SD) and traverse delay (TD) restrictions of each UE. To tackle this problem, we propose a deep reinforcement learning-based energy-efficient and mobility-aware SFC embedding strategy (DRL-EMSES). This strategy employs an SFC deployment controller to keep track of the conditions of network nodes in the SAGIN and make intelligent embedding decisions in response to placement and migration requests from network nodes. The proposed DRL-EMSES leverages a DRL technique, Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG), to handle the stochastic arrivals of placement and migration requests in the SFC deployment controller, thereby optimizing long-term network performance. The simulation results show that the proposed DRL-EMSES can successfully lower energy consumption of the SAGIN while meeting SD and TD restrictions of UEs.

title: Arc Behavior and Surface Evolution Under Targeted Current Conditioning
authors: Chaozheng Peng, Xiaoshe Zhai, Zhichao Chen, Haojie Jia, Shaoming Shi, Xiangzhe Li, Jianhua Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Plasma Science
abstract: The conditioning of vacuum interrupters (VIs) essentially involves a secondary metallurgical effect on contact surfaces induced by arc energy. The achievement of the following two objectives determines the final metallographic characteristics and conditioning efficacy: precise localization of insulation-weak areas on the contact surfaces to achieve uniform distribution of arc energy, and accurate regulation of energy deposition within the targeted regions to beneficially modify the contact surface. Since the breakdown conditions of the contact gap and the energy-injection conditions are coupled in conventional single-power-source modes, it is difficult to independently and effectively adjust the corresponding spatial and temporal parameters, thereby constraining further exploration of conditioning methodologies. To address the above issues, this article proposes a targeted current conditioning (TCC) technology that accurately locates and breaks down insulation weak spots using low-energy, high-voltage pulses. Subsequently, conditioning is realized by applying a high-current arc with specific amplitude and time constant to the identified region. This method overcomes the bottlenecks of uncontrollable arc energy and uneven energy distribution during conditioning, which can significantly enhance the insulation performance of VIs. Building on this foundation, this study uses high-speed imaging to observe the arc’s transfer characteristics and spatial distribution, which are correlated with field-emission characteristics to analyze the evolution of electrode surface properties. The main objectives of this study are: validating the feasibility of localization during targeted conditioning, revealing the intrinsic mechanisms underlying the evolution of surface features, and performing an in-depth investigation into the physical mechanisms responsible for insulation improvement under targeted current action.

title: An Electromechanical Coupling Model and Analysis for In-wheel Motor Considering Transient Multi-dimension Eccentricity
authors: Dejian Meng, Birui Li, Hongliang Ying, Zizhen Qiu, Lijun Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification
abstract: In-wheel motor (IWM) is a key trend in distributed drive electric vehicle powertrains. The increased unsprung mass, road surface, vehicle axle loads and unbalanced electromagnetic force (UEF) coupling induces IWM eccentricity. But it has not been verified by road tests, and eccentricity mechanism under road is unclear. In this research, both bench and road tests are designed, publicly reporting existence of IWM eccentricity under road. And multi-dimension eccentricity electromechanical coupling model (MDEEC) is proposed considering detailed IWM structural features. It consists of vertical dynamics model considering axial deflection (VDMAD) and UEF model with multi-dimension eccentricity (MDE). Through the study, mechanism of IWM eccentricity under road conditions is explained, which is caused by static MDE by vehicle axle loads and MDE fluctuations by np (n=1,2,3…, p represents the number of the pole pairs) order UEF. Besides, coupling between axial and vertical displacement is nonnegligible. Based on the results of MDEEC and the validation of tests, above factors lead to radial and axial deflection of 0.107 mm and 4.05×10-4 rad, which means eccentricity over 10% and leads to vibration deterioration. This research provides theoretical guidance for dynamics analysis and design of the IWM.

title: Stateless Per-Packet IoT Authentication Framework Using Machine Learning
authors: Nate Sherman, Mostafa El Said
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: Securing Internet of Things (IoT) devices remains a critical challenge due to constrained device resources, limited authentication mechanisms, and the lack of IoT-specific security datasets. This paper presents a stateless, per-packet authentication framework that integrates traditional TCP state validation with machine learning (ML) based classification, implemented using Microsoft Azure cloud services. A synthetic IoT network traffic generator was developed to address the scarcity of public datasets, enabling large-scale model training and controlled experimentation. Using Azure Machine Learning AutoML, a Voting Ensemble model achieved 96.9 % accuracy when trained on 200,000 simulated packets. The trained model was deployed within a real-time Azure pipeline incorporating IoT Central, Event Hubs, Azure Functions, and SQL Database services. Due to cloud resource constraints, live evaluation was conducted on approximately 450 packets, during which 64.4 % of traffic was filtered by TCP state validation prior to ML inference, demonstrating the effectiveness of multilayered security triage. Although latency was elevated because of nonproduction Azure components, the results validate the feasibility of combining lightweight protocol validation with machine learning to enhance IoT authentication. Beyond research contributions, the proposed framework provides a realistic, scalable environment testbed suitable for research and teaching cloud-based IoT and cybersecurity concepts.

title: Financial IoT Smishing Detection: Challenges, Gaps, and Research Roadmap
authors: Abit Jurkuch, Imad Mahgoub
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: Financial IoT (F-IoT) systems rely on SMS-based authentication for transaction alerts and access control, creating a high-value target for smishing attacks. While AI-based detection achieves high accuracy on benchmark datasets, production deployment faces three critical challenges: constrained edge device resources, adaptive adversaries, and stringent regulatory requirements. This paper provides a deployment-oriented analysis of classical machine learning, deep learning, and transformerbased methods through the lens of latency budgets, memory constraints, adversarial robustness, and regulatory compliance. We introduce a unified adversary model spanning device, edge, and cloud layers, mapping attack vectors including physical tampering, gateway compromise, data poisoning, and multitier threats. We formalize five operational assurance metrics beyond accuracy: asymmetric error costs, recovery latency, energy consumption, user friction, and update deployment. Our analysis reveals fundamental tradeoffs: classical ML achieves sub-millisecond inference but limited adversarial robustness; deep learning improves semantic understanding but exceeds device-tier constraints; transformers provide the strongest robustness but violate real-time requirements. Multi-tier architectures emerge as a necessary design pattern, distributing detection across device, edge, and cloud layers to satisfy competing constraints. We distill four design principles: robustness, adaptivity, efficiency, and trust, with technical mappings to GDPR, CCPA, and PCIDSS regulations. We identify critical gaps in certified defenses, rapid adaptation, privacy-preserving federated learning, and standardized benchmarks, and present a phased research roadmap with defined validation criteria.

title: Integrating Machine Learning and Deep Learning for Robust and Privacy-Preserving Intrusion Detection: A Unified Framework and Experimental Study
authors: Maha Ali Hussein, Sundos A. Hameed Alazawi, Haider K. Hoomod
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: An Intrusion Detection System (IDS) is still considered a necessary tool for ensuring the security of modern, highly distributed environments. However, the conventional methods have difficulty achieving all of the following: high detection accuracy, being robust against adversarial attacks, and ensuring privacy of data at the same time. Here, a synergistic hybrid solution that combines deep feature extraction via Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Bidirectional Long Short Term Memory (BiLSTM) with ensemble based classification using Random Forest is proposed by the authors. Moreover, the solution uses federated learning to achieve decentralized training without sharing the local raw network traffic among the parties. The experimental results on the CICIDS2017 dataset show statistically significant performance improvements compared to deep learning baseline models <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(p<0.01$</tex>) while obtaining a detection accuracy of 99.95% and very low variance over multiple runs. The authors have also subjected their model to FGSM, PGD, and C&W adversarial attacks and have observed that their model is more resilient than the single model counterparts. In addition, the cross dataset validation performed on UNSW NB15 confirms the generalization ability. The study results suggest that hybridizing representation learning, ensemble classification, and privacy preserving techniques yield a robust, practical, and stable intrusion detection system suitable for the present day network environments.

title: Intelligent Crop Protection: AI Powered Detection and Diagnosis of Plant Diseases
authors: Ankith Kumar, Ashray K, Ashith C, Adithya Maradithaya, Venugopala P S
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Agriculture is the main factor that supports India’s economy, but, the continuation of crop diseases cause significant losses of yields every year which threatens food security and farmers incomes. This research presents a smart, CNN-based system for the automatic identification and explanation of plant leaf diseases.The model was trained on a large and diverse dataset of more than 27000 images of 4 crops and 22 disease classes. By using state-of-the-art preprocessing and feature extraction, the CNN attained a validation accuracy of 92.57%, which is in line with high precision and strong generalization power. The trained model is housed in a Flask-based web application that offers instant disease identification along with easy-to-understand treatment suggestions, farm care instructions, soil management tips, and some care tips. By cutting the overuse of pesticides and promoting data-driven farming, the system is helping to sustain agriculture and increase crop yields. The next development will be an offline mobile version to provide more access and convenience in remote farming areas.

title: A Hybrid Stylometric and Transformer-Based Framework for Detecting AI-Generated Text
authors: Rimsha Kanwal, Qurat Ul Ain Zam Zam Mirza, Humaira Ashraf, NZ. Jhanjhi, Trisiani Dewi
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: The fast development of big models of language applications (LLMs) like ChatGPT, GPT-4, Claude, or Gemini has changed digital content development positively but has at the same time increased the difficulties in determining whether a certain text has been written by a human being or is produced by an artificial intelligence (AI). Online information, academic writing, and journalism take the risk of being compromised because of the indistinction between these two types of writing. In this paper, the hybrid architecture that combines stylometric and transformer-based embeddings is introduced to detect AIgenerated text correctly, particularly news and educational fields. The framework takes advantage of classic linguistic properties sentence length, frequency of punctuation, richness of lexicon and burstiness with the semantic representations based on the TF-IDF embeddings and BERT embeddings. Logistic Regression and Random Forest are the two supervised classifiers that were trained and tested with mixed datasets of human and AIgenerated texts. Experimental findings show that the Random Forest model was better in precision and robust and its average accuracy of 90% was higher than that of Logistic Regression (87%) with different samples. The proposed solution is effective because it can bridge the gap between interpretability and high performance, using both stylometric and semantic features. A comparative study with the current models in the GPTZero, GLTR and DetectGPT platforms shows better adaptability and automation, and this system is applicable to content verifiers, academic integrity systems, and news authenticity, which are used to examine the system. The future directions of the model include improvements to be multilingual data and multimodal detection (text and visual cues).

title: Proactive Approaches to Vulnerability Assessment and Mitigation
authors: Ethan Albrechjt, Dan Howard, Diego Vallejo, Graham Hewison, Tauheed Khan Mohd
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: As technology becomes more relied on in daily operations, the risks associated with software vulnerabilities such as data breaches, system failures, and financial losses have grown significantly. Traditional tools for detecting vulnerabilities, such as static and dynamic analysis, are quickly becoming inadequate due to the rapid pace of software development and the growing complexity of applications. This paper investigates new methods for identifying and categorizing vulnerabilities, focusing on the potential of machine learning and artificial intelligence to improve detection. These technologies can analyze large volumes of data and uncover patterns that might otherwise go unnoticed, helping to predict future vulnerabilities. In addition, our research highlights the challenges in patch management, highlighting the importance of automation in reducing human error and speeding up the deployment of patches. By exploring innovative tools and developing efficient workflows for vulnerability discovery and patching, this research aims to provide organizations with more effective strategies for minimizing security risks. The goal is to improve vulnerability management and help organizations better protect their systems from cyber threats.

title: A Trojan Attack on TDMA Synchronization in Energy-Harvesting Wireless Networks
authors: Ethan Berei, Alexander H. Burkholder, Ahmed Ammar
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: This paper investigates a Trojan attack targeting the time-division multiple access (TDMA) synchronization mechanism in single-hop energy-harvesting wireless networks. The attack compromises a single node, which subtly skews its transmission timing to operate outside its assigned time slot, causing localized transmission overlaps and triggering repeated networkwide resynchronization events. This behavior shortens the synchronization interval, significantly increases control-plane traffic, and leads to higher energy consumption and delay in energy-constrained networks. The attack is modeled within a finite state machine (FSM) framework and experimentally evaluated under varying energy-harvesting conditions. Experimental results show that the number of synchronization events can increase by up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$29 \times$</tex> in the presence of the Trojan. Furthermore, changes in the synchronization interval and its statistical variability are shown to be effective indicators of both severe and stealthy Trojan activity, highlighting the substantial impact of a low-footprint attack on network performance.

title: Credit Score Simulator and Coaching Module for Intelligent Credit
authors: Bhavan R, Pallavi Shetty
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: This study showcases a comprehensive system that merges credit score forecasting using machine learning with tailored financial coaching to enhance financial education for young adults. The system utilizes a fine-tuned RandomForest regression model developed on 100,000 financial records to estimate credit scores within the practical range of 300–850 (FICO scale) and produces clear, actionable suggestions via counterfactual simulation. The model attains an R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> of 0.4627 and an RMSE of 49.53 points, accounting for 46.27% of the variability in credit scores by utilizing 10 financial features. An innovative learned linear calibration method guarantees accurate score forecasts, while a greedy simulation technique offers tailored routes to achieve desired credit scores. The system tackles significant deficiencies in financial literacy by converting unclear credit scoring into transparent, instructive experiences that allow users to model "what-if" situations and comprehend the measurable effects of financial choices. The results of the validation show strong performance across various metrics, offering practical benefits for interventions in financial education and behavior change. This study adds to the expanding overlap of explainable AI, fintech, and edtech by showcasing how machine learning can be utilized not only for prediction, but also for enabling empowerment through comprehension.

title: A Multimodal Retrieval-Augmented Generation System for Intelligent Document Processing and Study Assistance
authors: Harish Kunder, Darshan C M, Sharan S Shetty, Sathyaprakash T, Shrihith S Poojary
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: This paper presents SmartNotes, a comprehensive multimodal document processing system that combines optical character recognition (OCR), retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and large language models (LLMs) to create an intelligent study assistant. The system processes diverse document formats (PDF, DOCX, images) including handwritten content, extracts knowledge through advanced OCR techniques, and enables contextual question-answering through a sophisticated vector search architecture. Our implementation features a novel hybrid OCR approach that combines TrOCR for handwriting recognition with Tesseract for printed text, a FAISS-based vector store for efficient similarity search, and Groq’s DeepSeek model for generative responses. The system additionally provides study tools including automated summarization, flashcard generation, and quiz creation. Experimental results demonstrate the system’s effectiveness in educational contexts, showing significant improvements in information retrieval accuracy and study efficiency compared to traditional methods. Our evaluation shows a 17% improvement in retrieval precision with cross-encoder reranking and a 48% improvement in answer accuracy compared to standalone LLMs. The system achieved an excellent System Usability Scale (SUS) score of 86/100 in user studies with 15 participants.

title: IoT and Machine Learning-Based Road Condition Monitoring and Pothole Detection System
authors: Harshitha M, Kavya Garg, Thanyaa S, Sheela S
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Potholes and uneven road surfaces have been identified as the leading causes of accidents, vehicle damage, and traffic inefficiency, particularly in developing countries such as India. Currently, manual inspection methods are not only inefficient but also do not have the capability of real-time reporting. This article introduces an IoT-based Pothole and Road Condition Monitoring (RCM) System combined with Machine Learning (ML) for automatic localization and classification of road surface conditions. The system is based on an ESP32 microcontroller equipped with an ultrasonic sensor, accelerometer (MPU6050), and GPS module that enables live road data collection. A Random Forest Classifier trained on a 10,000-sample synthetic dataset modeled for Indian road conditions was able to classify the road as pothole, rough road, speed bump, or smooth road with an accuracy of 84.7%. Real-time data transmission and GPS mapping allow for the immediate visualization of the damaged areas. This system delivers an affordable, scalable, and intelligent solution to smart city road maintenance and safety.

title: Sea-Trial Characterization of the Spatial Self-Interference in In-Band Full-Duplex Underwater Acoustic Systems: A Validated Finite-Element Model and Receive-Array Design
authors: Xuechao Niu, Xin Qing, Yuncong Wang, Tengjiao He, Gang Qiao, Yixin Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: In this paper, we develop an in-band full-duplex underwater acoustic modem as a measurement platform and report sea-trial measurements to characterize spatial self-interference (SI) and to validate SI suppression performance under practical deployment constraints. Due to the strong SI power and the ultra-short SI propagation path, SI can severely degrade the detection and characterization of weak signals in practical deployments; therefore, accurate SI modeling and field measurements are essential for reliable detection and measurement-informed suppression. Thus, we develop a finite-element-method based numerical SI model that incorporates complex mechanical structures and realistic transducer radiation characteristics. To mitigate the performance degradation of adaptive beamformers in detection caused by strong power SI, we propose a quasi-nested receive array. Numerical simulations and sea-trial measurements show that, for a seven-element array, the proposed quasi-nested array achieves the highest output signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) among the compared arrays, with gains of up to 11 dB in simulations and 8.7 dB in sea trials over the uniform linear array (ULA). In addition, simulations under SI-field uncertainty confirm that the quasi-nested array maintains the highest output SINR among the compared array configurations.

title: Two-Stage Delay-Doppler Aided 6D Beamspace Tensor Channel Estimation for High-Mobility mmWave MIMO-OFDM
authors: Tianyu Jiang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: Accurate channel estimation in high-mobility millimeter-wave (mmWave) multiple-input multiple-output orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing (MIMO-OFDM) systems with hybrid beamforming is challenging due to severe Doppler and limited training overhead. We use a beamspace-frequency-time tensor representation to organize the channel over four spatial beamspace modes, the OFDM subcarrier mode, and block-level time variation. Based on this representation, we develop a two-stage estimator that decouples time-frequency and spatial processing: Stage 1 exploits Vandermonde shift-invariance and applies an ESPRIT-type subspace method to estimate the dominant delay-Doppler parameters from a compact time-frequency data matrix, and Stage 2 compensates the estimated delay-Doppler factors and reconstructs the 4D spatial beamspace tensor via convex multi-mode nuclear-norm regularized low-rank recovery. Simulations demonstrate consistent normalized mean-squared error gains over representative baselines in high-mobility scenarios.

title: MMIFN: A Multi-Modal Interactive Fusion Network for Omnidirectional Image Quality Assessment
authors: Yun Liu, Sifan Li, Huiyu Duan, Daoxin Fan, Guangtao Zhai
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Omnidirectional image quality assessment (OIQA) has attracted increasing attention in recent years, but is still challenging due to its complexity. Most earlier works are built based on visual degradation-aware features from the entire distorted image and/or the viewports, which focus on extracting representative visual features. Unlike conventional OIQA methods that only rely on visual information, we propose an effective multimodal interactive fusion network for OIQA with the help of textual semantic information, which is conceptually inspired by the non-declarative and declarative memory cognition mechanism in human brain. Specifically, the text information is firstly captured based on the available visual modality to get a detailed and accurate quality description, which can provide rich declarative semantic information, and is complemented with the non-declarative visual information. Then the textual semantic information is not only fused with the global visual features at various levels but also fused with the local visual features, following human view characteristics of an omnidirectional image. Finally, an aggregated integrated module is designed to further strengthen the feature fusion, which digs into the deep image-text interaction relation and improves the text-image fusion accuracy. Extensive experiments on two public datasets prove the effectiveness of our model and the benefit of multimodal information in OIQA field.

title: Edge-Wise Lie-Group Masking for Secure and Opaque Consensus
authors: Camilla Fioravanti, D. H. S. Maithripala, Gabriele Oliva
publish: IEEE Control Systems Letters
abstract: Ensuring consensus while preventing the leakage of sensitive information to external observers is a fundamental challenge in networked multi-agent systems. This paper proposes a mechanism for masking the communication level of a network that performs consensus, based on Lie group actions. Specifically, agents evolve in the usual Euclidean space and perform a standard discrete-time linear consensus update, while each transmitted message is transformed by a group action and, for each edge, by a private key shared exclusively by the endpoint agents. According to this protocol, legitimate agents can reverse the transformation and recover the exact states of their neighbors, thereby ensuring that convergence to the average is not affected. An eavesdropper, on the other hand, can only observe masked messages expressed in unknown, edge-dependent coordinate systems. We prove that, even with full knowledge of the protocol and complete interception of all links, the states of the agents are unidentifiable beyond the action invariants, i.e., the intercepted traffic is compatible with an entire family of internal trajectories up to orbit invariants. Simulations illustrate how static and time-varying keys model what an external observer can and cannot infer from masked communications.

title: Multi-Scale Residual Shrinkage Network with Adaptive Thresholding for Noise-Robust EEG Sleep Staging
authors: Jingyi Lin, Jinbiao Zhang, Jin Cheng
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Reliable automatic sleep staging from single-channel electroencephalography (EEG) is critical for scalable diagnosis of sleep disorders, yet clinical deployment remains limited by EEG artifact contamination, the restricted receptive field of single-scale convolutional networks, and performance degradation across subjects. We propose MS-IDRSN, a Multi-Scale Improved Deep Residual Shrinkage Network that jointly addresses these three challenges through: (1) an adaptive threshold function whose bias decays exponentially with signal amplitude, resolving the bias–discontinuity tradeoff inherent in classical shrinkage estimators while maintaining continuity and gradient stability; (2) three parallel convolutional branches with kernel sizes of 3, 5, and 7 that capture sleep-relevant oscillations spanning spindle cycles to delta half-waves at 100 Hz; and (3) a Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD)-based domain alignment module that reduces inter-subject feature divergence in both within-dataset and cross-dataset settings without requiring target labels. Evaluated on Sleep-EDFx (78 subjects) and HMC (151 subjects), the model achieves overall accuracies of 92.78% and 91.89%, with N1 stage recall of 78.0%—a 12.3 percentage-point improvement over the single-scale DRSN baseline. Under additive white Gaussian noise at −6 dB, the model retains 87.45% accuracy. Ablation experiments confirm that each component contributes independently, and all seven pairwise baseline comparisons reach Bonferroni-corrected p < 0.001 across 15 fold-seed runs with 15/15 win rates. These results demonstrate that combining multi-scale feature extraction with adaptive denoising and domain adaptation yields a practical, noise-tolerant framework suitable for automated clinical sleep analysis.

title: 3D Reflectarray Antenna Design for Ku Application using 3D Printer Technology
authors: Ladislau Matekovits, Furkan Başkır, Hasan Kıyık, Aysu Belen, Tarlan Mahouti, Mehmet A. Belen
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This paper presents the design, fabrication, and experimental validation of a Ku-band 3D-printed reflectarray (RA) antenna established on a quasi-periodic arrangement, operating at 13 GHz. The proposed grounded dielectric only 3D unit cell consists of a polylactic acid (PLA)-based multilayer structure incorporating a constant height prism on top of which a variable height square prism resonator is located; such a configuration is maintained at a constant distant from the ground plane through and an air spacer. The arrangement enables continuous reflection-phase control through geometry and material parameter tuning. A systematic parametric analysis is conducted to quantify the influence of air-gap thickness, dielectric thickness, relative permittivity, and resonator dimensions on the reflection-phase response. The results demonstrate that the air spacer provides the dominant phase-adjustment mechanism, while the remaining parameters enable fine-resolution phase compensation, forming a practical and fabrication-aware phase-synthesis strategy. Using full-wave–generated phase mapping and classical RA compensation principles, a 16 × 16 array comprising 256 elements and of extension of 240 × 240 mm² is synthesized. Numerical simulations predict a realized gain of 21.4 dBi at 13 GHz with controlled sidelobe levels and stable polarization characteristics. A prototype has been fabricated using additive manufacturing technology and experimentally characterized in a horn-fed measurement setup. The measured radiation characteristics show good agreement with simulations, validating both the unit-cell model and the aperture-level phase distribution methodology. The proposed approach demonstrates that low-cost 3D-printing technology can realize high-gain Ku-band RAs without multilayer stacking or complex fabrication processes. Owing to its scalability, lightweight structure, and compatibility with tunable elements, the presented design framework offers a promising pathway toward broadband, reconfigurable RAs and next-generation intelligent reflecting surfaces for satellite and high-frequency wireless communication applications.

title: A Worm-like Robot for Ferromagnetic Surface Inspection_supp3-3683248.mp4
authors: Rezvan Nasiri
publish: N/A
abstract: Industrial inspection is a crucial task for ensuring the longterm maintenance of infrastructures with ferromagnetic surfaces, such as power plants, wind turbines, and chemical facilities. These environments are often unsafe for human access, motivating the use of climbing robots to perform inspection tasks reliably and repeatedly; nevertheless, the existing robots often have limited maneuverability, reduced stability during transitions, and high energy consumption. In this article, we present a novel robotic system inspired by the body morphology of caterpillars. The design consists of three modules connected via a spring joint mechanism and equipped with a passive magnetic adhesion system. The design’s maneuverability, stability, energy efficiency, and falling robustness are studied analytically and experimentally. Experimental results confirmed the robot’s ability to perform internal/external corner transitions, thin surface transitions, pipe circling, and surface turning in real-world environments, including rusty, coated, and uneven surfaces. Furthermore, we found that compared with simple locomotion, the designed wormlike locomotion yielded an average 10.2% increase in forward velocity and 6.7% reduction in the cost of transportation (COT). These findings suggest that the proposed design offers a practical and versatile approach for inspecting ferromagnetic surfaces.

title: Neural Representation Learning and Privacy-preserving Cloud-Edge Collaboration for Next-Generation Financial Wealth Management Platforms
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: Wealth management must be innovative, private, and transparent as financial ecosystems become more complicated. NeuroWealth, a neural representation and privacy-aware framework for next-generation financial systems, integrates graph neural networks (GNNs), transformer-based temporal learning, and federated cloud-edge collaboration. To provide accurate, adaptable, and explainable investment suggestions, the system records relational and temporal relationships among people, institutions, and goods. NeuroWealth solves new client and product cold-start issues with meta-learning (MAML) and content-collaborative embeddings. The methodology ensures GDPR and MiFID II model interpretability and compliance via SHAP-based explainability and counterfactual reasoning. Federated learning (FL) with differential privacy (DP) protects sensitive financial data while retaining usefulness. Experiments on actual and synthetic datasets show 17.6% improvement in NDCG, 31 % reduction in cold-start error, and 0.84-0.92 compliance with essential regulations in terms of explainability. Live benchmarks show <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 2 0\ m s}$</tex> latency and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 5, 0 0 0}$</tex> requests/sec throughput, showing NeuroWealth's large-scale viability. The platform provides scalable, safe, and interpretable AI for personalised financial wealth management.

title: K-Means Based Tinyml Anomaly Detection and Distributed Model Reuse via the Distributed Internet of Learning (DIoL)
authors: Abdulrahman Albaiz, Fathi Amsaad
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: This paper presents a lightweight K-Means anomaly detection model and a distributed model-sharing workflow designed for resource-constrained microcontrollers (MCUs). Using real power measurements from a mini-fridge appliance, the system performs on-device feature extraction, clustering, and threshold estimation to identify abnormal appliance behavior. To avoid retraining models on every device, we introduce the Distributed Internet of Learning (DIoL), which enables a model trained on one MCU to be exported as a portable, text-based representation and reused directly on other devices. A two-device prototype demonstrates the feasibility of the “Train Once, Share Everywhere” (TOSE) approach using a real-world appliance case study, where Device A trains the model and Device B performs inference without retraining. Experimental results show consistent anomaly detection behavior, negligible parsing overhead, and identical inference runtimes between standalone and DIoL-based operation. The proposed framework enables scalable, low-cost TinyML deployment across fleets of embedded devices.

title: Traffic Routing & Prediction Using Optimized ACO-PSO
authors: Bhakthi S Shervegar, Pallavi Shetty
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Intelligent routing solutions that can react to dynamic congestion are necessary for modern urban traffic systems. In order to calculate congestion-aware traffic routes, this project offers a hybrid optimization framework that combines Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) and Ant Colony Optimization (ACO). Road length, inflow, outflow, and lane capacity are all taken into account by the model's Road Congestion Factor (RCF). To improve route optimality and speed up convergence, a PSO module adjusts ACO parameters. A real-time, user-interactive visual platform that forecasts traffic flow and shows the best routes via an SVG-based map is made possible by the system's Flask deployment. When compared to the baseline ACO method, the suggested hybrid ACO PSO model shows better path quality and shorter computation times.

title: Context-Aware Semantic Classification of Digital News Headlines Using Transformer-Based Models
authors: Anusha, Manjula Shenoy K, Smitha N Pai
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: The rapid expansion of digital news content necessitates the development of effective classification systems. Traditional news classification relied on standard models such as Random Forest, Support Vector Machines (SVM), and Naive Bayes. Advanced transformer models such as BERT, RoBERTa, and DistilBERT have improved Natural Language Processing (NLP) by utilizing advanced contextual understanding and self-supervised learning. This study explores the application of BERT, RoBERTa, and DistilBERT for categorizing digital news titles based on the context. The transformer models were trained, tested, and validated using an open-source dataset that includes news headlines and their categories, such as business, entertainment, health, general headline, science, sports, technology, and global news. A comparative analysis reveals that the DistilBERT model outperforms the RoBERTa and BERT models in terms of classification accuracy while requiring less training time compared to the BERT and RoBERTa models.

title: An Improved SMS Security Technique to Avoid Plaintext and Dictionary Attacks
authors: Qurat Ul Ain Zam Zam Mirza, Humaira Ashraf, Rimsha Kanwal, NZ. Jhanjhi, Hermanto
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: In the era of flourishing digital communication and the unprecedented surge in mobile device usage, securing message transmission has become paramount. The prevalence of short messaging services (SMS) intensifies the demand for robust security measures to safeguard the privacy of smartphone users. “Security” is a crucial component in this kind of circumstance. Encryption is one method that's frequently utilized to offer security. It protects the original raw data from in-trusion and plays a crucial function when private data is moving via the network. This research introduces an innovative approach to DNA Cryptography, leveraging a dynamic DNA sequence table to enhance security in SMS communications. The proposed Efficient Cryptographic Scheme for SMS (ECSS) algorithm employs DNA molecules and strands to encrypt information, ensuring a high level of confidentiality for texts received on Android smartphones. ECSS cryptography focuses on preserving the secrecy of data transfer over networks. The paper explores the nuances of DNA Crypto, distinguishing it from classical cryptography and elucidating the process of DNA Data encryption. An Androidbased application is developed to implement the proposed technique, demonstrating consistent encryption results where the length of the message remains unchanged after decryption. The security solution exhibits potential for significantly enhancing mobile communication security. The study also presents experimental results, including received text message accuracy, cipher text, average encryption time, and average decryption time. The findings reveal an average encryption and decryption speed of approximately 0.011 and 0.037 seconds, respectively, effectively thwarting plaintext and dictionary attacks.

title: Secure and Privacy-Preserving Frameworks for IoMT: A Survey of Cryptographic and Blockchain-Enabled Approaches
authors: Hussien AbdelRaouf, Ahmed T. Eltoukhy, Mostafa Fouda, Zubair Md Fadlullah, Mohamed I. Ibrahem
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: The Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) is changing how care is delivered through continuous sensing and analytics. However, it also increases the risk of attacks across devices, edge, and cloud. This survey summarizes the current state of end-to-end privacy and security for IoMT, organized by the data lifecycle: capture, storage and sharing, query and analytics, and learning. We examine lightweight device-side encryption, such as chaos-driven keystreams, that provides real-time protection for constrained wearables. We also examine edge architectures that combine cryptographic techniques with decentralized off-chain storage and blockchain smart contracts to ensure integrity, access control, and auditability. Additionally, we explore ciphertext-friendly analytics that leverage additive homomorphic computation, symmetric searchable encryption, and Bloom-filter indexing. Finally, we discuss privacy-preserving intelligence through federated learning (FL) with semantically secure update sharing. In each area, we evaluate performance trade-offs in computation, latency, scalability, and trust distribution. The survey offers a classification of mechanisms, a comparison matrix of guarantees and costs, and a reference architecture that combines these techniques into a clear, regulation-compliant stack.

title: A Jitter-Driven Hardware Hybrid Random Number Generator (HHRNG) Using Nonlinear Feedback and Feedforward Processing
authors: Sonia Akter, Kasem Khalil, Magdy Bayoumi
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: In this complex, ubiquitous communication era, all digital communication demands secure key generation for cryptographic protocols, which in turn relies on high-quality random number generation. There are two main categories of RNGs: pseudo-random number generators (PRNGs) and True Random Number generators (TRNGs). Even though TRNGs provide non-deterministic, unpredictable outputs, they often require complex components to extract the bitstream from physical phenomena and are sensitive to environmental variations. PRNGs, however, are deterministic and require a seed to initiate the operation, and are often considered unsuitable for high-security application due to shorter cycle lengths and correlation. However, PRNGs, for example, Non Linear feedback Shift register (NLFSR), are easy to implement and provide better statistical properties such as good balances of zeros and ones required for a high-quality RNG, as they are mathematical equation-based. In this work, a robust HHRNG is proposed by mutually reinforcing the NLFSR with Jitter from Ring Oscillators (ROs) for reliable and unpredictable random number generation. Moreover, the feedforward architecture, along with dynamic Multiplexer (MUX) selection, adds another two layers of unpredictability. The proposed HRNG implemented on Intel Cyclone V FPGA, consuming under 1 % of logic resources at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 0 M H z}$</tex>. Moreover, the collected more than one million bits sequence passed rigorous NIST SP800-22 and NIST SP800-90B non-IID tests. The Shannon entropy calculation presents near-ideal entropy of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.999999(\approx 1.00)$</tex> and a bias of only 0.11 %, indicating excellent statistical quality and unpredictability applicable for lightweight security-critical applications.

title: AaSHM: Unsupervised Bearing Fault Detection Using an Anomaly-Aware Spatiotemporal Hypergraph
authors: Yan Liu
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Effective bearing health monitoring is critical for industrial predictive maintenance, yet it remains challenging due to the scarcity of labeled fault data and complex spatiotemporal dependencies in multi-channel sensor signals. To address these issues, this paper proposes an Unsupervised Anomaly-aware Spatiotemporal Hypergraph Model (AaSHM) for bearing fault detection. AaSHM learns normal operating patterns from healthy data and identifies potential faults through anomaly scoring during inference. The proposed model employs a dynamic local-global hypergraph structure to capture high-order spatial interactions among sensor channels and long-range temporal dependencies: local hyperedges are constructed using multi-source statistical features to characterize fine-grained sensor couplings, while global hyperedges model system-level correlations based on mutual information. Furthermore, an anomaly-gating mechanism is integrated into the hypergraph convolution process to adaptively modulate feature aggregation based on node-level anomaly characteristics, enhancing sensitivity to incipient faults. Extensive experiments on the CWRU and PU benchmark datasets demonstrate that AaSHM achieves superior comprehensive performance. Specifically, AaSHM consistently outperforms existing graph-based and deep learning baselines in F1-score, AUC, and AUPR across various operating conditions.
