title: Cross-Scenario Deraining Adaptation with Unpaired Data: Superpixel Structural Priors and Multi-Stage Pseudo-Rain Synthesize
authors: Kangbo Zhao, Miaoxin Guan, Xiang Chen, Yukai Shi, Jinshan Pan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Image deraining plays a pivotal role in low-level computer vision, serving as a prerequisite for robust outdoor surveillance and autonomous driving systems. While deep learning paradigms have achieved remarkable success in firmly aligned settings, they often suffer from severe performance degradation when generalized to unseen Out-of-Distribution (OOD) scenarios. This failure stems primarily from the significant domain discrepancy between synthetic training datasets and the complex physical dynamics of real-world rain. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a pioneering cross-scenario deraining adaptation framework. Diverging from conventional approaches, our method obviates the requirements for paired rainy observations in the target domain, leveraging exclusively rain-free background images. We design a Superpixel Generation (Sup-Gen) module to extract stable structural priors from the source domain using Simple Linear Iterative Clustering. Subsequently, a Resolution-adaptive Fusion strategy is introduced to align these source structures with target backgrounds through texture similarity, ensuring the synthesis of diverse and realistic pseudo-data. Finally, we implement a pseudo-label re-Synthesize mechanism that employs multi-stage noise generation to simulate realistic rain streaks. This framework functions as a versatile plug-and-play module capable of seamless integration into arbitrary deraining architectures. Extensive experiments on state-of-the-art models demonstrate that our approach yields remarkable PSNR gains of up to 32% to 59% in OOD domains while significantly accelerating training convergence.

title: Multi-View Depth Estimation With Uncertainty Constraints for Virtual-Real Occlusion
authors: Jia Liu, Bin Wang, Dapeng Chen, Hong Song, Zengwei Zhang, Nanxuan Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: In the context of the rapid development of Augmented Reality (AR) technology, addressing the challenge of occlusion between virtual objects and real scenes becomes increasingly important. This paper focuses on depth-based occlusion handling methods for AR applications and proposes a multi-view depth estimation model. To improve the accuracy of object depth contour estimation, a Bayesian Convolutional Uncertainty Estimation module is incorporated into the model, and a new uncertainty loss function is introduced to constrain depth contours. Additionally, an Attention Spatial Convolution Fusion module is integrated into the feature encoder to enhance feature representation capability. Finally, an optional post-processing method, the Depth Edge Padding module, is provided. This module aims to optimize the performance of the depth map output by the model at object edges. The model demonstrates superior depth estimation performance on the ScanNet and 7Scenes datasets compared to existing multi-view depth estimation research, particularly exhibiting higher accuracy in object contour regions. Moreover, the model also exhibits more realistic effects in occlusion handling between virtual and real scenes.

title: 3D Localization of In-Service Deep-Buried PE Pipelines via ELF Magnetic Matching: System Design and Field Validation
authors: Mingrui Zhang, Ke Liu, Ningfeng Zhang, Junzhe Shi, Jinyu Ma, Xinjing Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: The non-conductive and non-magnetic properties of polyethylene (PE), combined with the severe attenuation of acoustic and electromagnetic waves by soil, render 3D localization of deep-buried, in-service PE gas pipelines a significant challenge. This study presents a 3D localization system based on extremely low frequency (ELF) magnetic field matching, consisting of a high-current ground magnetic station and a Φ17 mm miniature spherical detector (SD). The station generates penetrative ELF fields through large wire loops, while the SD is deployed through existing purge valves without interrupting gas service. Trajectory reconstruction is achieved by matching the magnetic fields measured by the SD to a precomputed field map under kinematic constraints, rather than by relying on long-distance inertial dead reckoning. Furthermore, system scaling laws are established, demonstrating that localization uncertainty can be reduced by increasing wire loop current and employing large-scale wire loops to maintain deep-buried pipelines within the magnetic near-field region. In field tests on a 140 m PE pipeline buried at about 4.5 m depth, repeated localization experiments were conducted. The deviation between the mean of three repeated runs and the commercial SINS results is 0.32 m, with a repeatability precision of 0.07 m. These results verify the system’s effectiveness for high-precision, nondestructive localization of buried urban in-service PE gas pipelines.

title: A Deep Neural Network-Based Adaptive Gamification Approach for Virtual Learning_supp1-3700399.docx
authors: Feng Chen
publish: N/A
abstract: Gamification learning in virtual reality (VR) environments is extensively utilized to stimulate learner motivation and enhance engagement. However, most existing gamification approaches rely on manually predefined rules or static matching, limiting their adaptability to dynamic changes in learning motivation and behaviors throughout the learning process. Accordingly, we propose a Deep Neural Network-based Adaptive Gamification for Virtual Learning (DAG-VL) method and integrate it into an instructional objective-oriented virtual learning pedagogical framework to facilitate adaptive gamification. Within this framework, structured instructional activities are designed according to predefined instructional objectives, and learner feedback data drive the adaptive gamification mechanism, which invokes DAG-VL to dynamically optimize gamification element configurations for subsequent activities. By integrating individualized incentives into the learning process, the mechanism strengthens learner autonomy and competence, thereby encouraging active interaction and improving learning outcomes. DAG-VL incorporates Hexad user types (a learner profiling model categorizing learners into six motivational orientations), alongside learning behavioral characteristics and task contextual information as input to train an end-to-end neural network model, enabling the automatic capture of nonlinear relationships between learner characteristics and learning tasks, facilitating dynamic recommendation of gamification elements. Experimental results indicate that DAG VL exhibits significant advantages in improving academic performance, promoting the practical application of knowledge, and providing a positive user experience. This study offers a reusable framework and implementation pathway for adaptive gamification instructional design in virtual learning.

title: Real-time Measurement of Transition Resistance and Full-time Fault Feeder Selection for Random Arc Fault in Active Distribution Networks
authors: Baowen Liu, Zhenguo Xiang, Xiangjun Zeng, Zixiong Wan, Jiayi Xiao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Smart Grid
abstract: Single-phase-to-ground fault occurs frequently in active distribution network, often accompanied by nonlinear arc, and the fault development trend changes instantaneously and randomly. However, the existing fault detection methods for stable faults or specific arc faults are difficult to work in practical fault conditions. To achieve real-time tracking of the fault development trend, precise identification of fault types, and reliable selection of fault feeder for random faults occurring in complex and diverse environments, a novel method for real-time measurement of transition resistance and full-time fault feeder selection is proposed in this paper. Firstly, an equivalent circuit diagram of the active distribution network is established considering the parameter asymmetry and damping ratio, to acquire the current flowing through the transition resistance and the voltage across it in real time. Subsequently, the transition resistances of various types of faults are measured in real time based on Ohm’s law, and the stability, periodicity and mutation characteristics of the measured transition resistance are utilized to accurately identify the fault type and track its development trend. Finally, the zero-sequence current is decomposed to eliminate, as much as possible, components irrelevant to fault feeder selection. This process ensures that the characteristic waveforms of the fault feeder and normal feeders remain in opposite phases throughout the entire time period, thereby preventing selection failures caused by the loss of critical transient information. Simulation and 10kV experimental platform prove the feasibility of the proposed methods.

title: Learning Optimal Crew Dispatch for Grid Restoration Following an Earthquake
authors: Farshad Amani, Faezeh Ardali, Amin Kargarian
publish: IEEE Transactions on Smart Grid
abstract: Post-earthquake crew dispatch is critical for distribution-system restoration, but is computationally demanding at operational scale. Classical mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) formulations can require minutes to hours to obtain high-quality solutions, thereby delaying field actions and degrading recovery performance in time-sensitive settings. This paper proposes a learning-based framework that couples a Transformer state encoder with a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) policy to enable near-real-time dispatch decisions. The problem is modeled as a sequential decision process with time-evolving system states, multi-crew coupling, and routing/repair-time constraints. Earthquake-induced component damage is characterized using established seismic standards, and a scenario generation and screening pipeline produces a compact geospatial impact representation for dispatch. The policy is trained offline and deployed online via a single forward pass with feasibility masking, after which power-flow re-solving is used to quantify time-varying curtailed load. Restoration performance is evaluated using instantaneous curtailed load, cumulative curtailed energy ENScum, and the resilience score RAUC. Case studies on 13-, 123-, and 2869-bus systems show that the proposed method achieves second-level decision times and up to 99.7% runtime reduction compared with CPLEX, Gurobi, and a genetic algorithm baseline, while improving recovery quality (lower ENScum and higher RAUC). An ablation study against non-attention DRL baselines further confirms the measurable benefit of the Transformer representation.

title: High-Order CBF-Compatible Semantics and Hierarchical Synthesis for Bounded-Time STL
authors: Chao Tang, Penghong Lu, Zhenpeng Lao, Gang Chen
publish: IEEE Control Systems Letters
abstract: This letter studies bounded-time signal temporal logic (STL) control for nonlinear systems whose output predicates have finite relative degree. We augment the plant with a deterministic bounded-time monitor and derivative-chain coordinates, obtaining a lifted state on which high-order CBF constraints enforce monitor-indexed stay and target tubes. Exactness is not claimed for arbitrary HOCBF sets; it is localized to a certified atomic class in which the lifted barrier-compatible set equals the one-step atomic winning set. For atoms certified in this class and a reset-consistent finite monitor, exactness propagates through Boolean and bounded-time temporal operators. A sampled-data hierarchical HOCBF-QP then minimizes sampled semantic slack before reference tracking. Under strict tube-wise feasibility and an intersample-preservation margin, recursive feasibility and conditional exact satisfaction are established for the certified subclass. A double-integrator study illustrates the online implementation and baseline separation, without using the experiment as a proof of the atomic equality certificate.

title: Physics-Informed Learning for Multiplayer Differential Games
authors: Takuma Adams, Andrew C. Cullen, Tansu Alpcan
publish: IEEE Control Systems Letters
abstract: Computing per-player feedback strategies in N-player adversarial differential games is computationally challenging as the associated coupled Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman system is high-dimensional and generally lacks classical solutions. We address this by approximating viscosity solutions using physics-informed neural networks within a policy iteration scheme with a vanishing-viscosity annealing schedule. Theoretically, we establish an explicit ε-Nash guarantee by bounding per-player regret via computable residuals and prove our method reaches this stopping criterion in finite time. For two-player zero-sum games, we further establish a computable bound on approximation error for the value functions. Numerical experiments on pursuit-evasion scenarios illustrate the tightness of these computed bounds and the framework’s scalability to high-dimensional environments.

title: BhashaBluff: A Dataset and Benchmark for Detecting Bilingual, Noisy, Compressed Deepfakes and Synthetic Audio
authors: Rishabh Ranjan, Mayank Vatsa, Richa Singh
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biometrics, Behavior, and Identity Science
abstract: Robust detection methods are essential to address the growing threat of audio deepfakes and synthetic media, which are increasingly appearing in complex, real-world conditions. Existing research has primarily focused on clean, single-language audio, overlooking critical challenges like bilingualism, background noise, and various forms of audio codec compression. This paper introduces the BhashaBluff dataset, with approximately 2,500 hours of audio data and over 3.88 million samples generated using 21 distinct methods. Its key innovation lies in its extensive variability across three critical dimensions: (i) bilingualism, which encompasses Hindi and English deepfakes as well as code-mixed speech; (ii) noise, with samples corrupted by four distinct environmental noise types; and (iii) compression, incorporating seven neural compression techniques to simulate real-world artifacts. Benchmark evaluations using state-of-the-art models show substantial performance degradation when faced with these challenging conditions, revealing the limited generalizability of current methods and highlighting the urgent need for more adaptive and robust algorithms. BhashaBluff is publicly available<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup> and serves as a crucial benchmark for developing next-generation detection systems that are effective in diverse, noisy, and compressed audio environments.

title: Real-Time Radar Hand Motion Recognition Using a Hybrid CNN–SVM Framework With FPGA Acceleration
authors: Taher S.Ahmed, Ahmed Fathy, Magdy Elbahnasawy, Peter F. Driessen, Ahmed Youssef
publish: IEEE Transactions on Radar Systems
abstract: Radar-based hand gesture recognition (HGR) systems often suffer from challenges related to sensor interference, environmental clutter, and limited dataset size, which complicate the training of deep learning models. This paper presents an efficient radar-based HGR framework that integrates radar signal preprocessing, deep learning-based feature extraction, and hardware acceleration on a System-on-Chip (SoC) FPGA platform. To investigate the impact of radar data representation on classification performance, two experimental pipelines are explored: an image-based radar representation and a continuous-valued range–time map (CV-RTM). The preprocessing stage includes clutter suppression, squared-magnitude transformation, Gaussian filtering, and normalization to enhance gesture-related radar signatures. For the image-based pipeline, binarized radar gesture images are used to train a hybrid convolutional neural network– support vector machine (CNN–SVM) classifier. The proposed CNN–SVM model achieves a CNN training accuracy of 93.3% compared to 92.2% for a CNN–LSTM–SVM architecture, while reducing model parameters by 75.5% and decreasing SVM training and prediction times by 29.6% and 32.6%, respectively. For the CV-RTM pipeline, multiple deep learning configurations are evaluated under varying signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) conditions using a curriculum learning strategy to improve robustness in noisy radar environments. Experimental results demonstrate that the combined CNN–SVM model with curriculum learning achieves the most stable performance across SNR levels ranging from −20 dB to 10 dB. Finally, the optimized model is deployed on a Xilinx Kria KR260 FPGA platform using the Vitis-AI framework. The hardware implementation achieves real-time inference with significant improvements in execution speed and throughput, confirming the suitability of the proposed framework for embedded radar gesture recognition applications.

title: Decentralized and Lightweight Server-Aided Revocable Attribute-Based Encryption for Mobile Cloud Computing_supp1-3696736.pdf
authors: Arthur Sandor Voundi Koe
publish: N/A
abstract: Mobile cloud computing (MCC) optimizes storage and computation on resource-limited mobile devices by leveraging heterogeneous networks and robust cloud servers. However, outsourcing local data to cloud servers, which may be malicious, also raises concerns about data privacy. As a prominent solution for securing outsourced data, server-aided revocable attribute-based encryption (SR-ABE) enforces fine-grained access control over encrypted data while supporting lightweight user revocation. Yet, available SR-ABE schemes struggle in practice due to the following reasons. First, they rely on a fully trusted key generation center to manage attributes and issue long-term transformation and decryption keys. Second, they require setting up a fixed number of data users, and overlook attribute revocation. Third, they incorporate time-based revocation mechanisms that assume synchrony in the underlying network, which does not always hold in practice. This paper presents a secure and practical SR-ABE scheme for MCC that supports the decentralized generation of long-term user keys while integrating state-triggered user and attribute revocation mechanisms. The proposed SR-ABE scheme accommodates unlimited data users, minimizes the sizes of key and ciphertext updates via lazy revocation, and ensures verifiable outsourced decryption. We formally prove that the proposed SR-ABE scheme is adaptively secure, and conduct extensive experiments demonstrating its practicality.

title: Adaptive Sensor Fault-Tolerant Tracking Control for State-Constrained Uncertain Nonlinear Systems
authors: Jintao Zhang, Donghua Zhou, Li Sheng, Junxing Che, Yanzheng Zhu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: In this paper, an adaptive sensor fault-tolerant tracking control scheme is developed for state-constrained uncertain nonlinear systems. Unlike most existing methods that directly rely on fault-contaminated sensor measurements for feedback control, an adaptive fault-compensated estimator (AFCE) is developed. By embedding an adaptive compensation term into the estimator feedback channel, the AFCE dynamically counteracts sensor faults and enables the joint estimation of system states and external disturbances. To accommodate state constraints, a generalized intermittent state constraint (GISC) approach is introduced. By constructing a novel switching function and an auxiliary variable, the proposed GISC achieves smooth transitions between constrained and unconstrained phases, while eliminating the conventional requirement that the upper and lower bounds have opposite signs. Then, building upon the estimated results, a generalized barrier Lyapunov function is incorporated into the fault-tolerant controller design. Rigorous Lyapunov analysis proves that the actual system output can track the desired reference while all states remain within the prescribed bounds under the established controller. Finally, the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed approach are verified through both simulation and experimental results.

title: Mental Health Prediction System Using Artificial Intelligence
authors: Manjula K Shenoy, Sachin Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Mental health is an important element of human’s well-being. This is a project that demonstrates a prediction system integrating Machine Learning (ML) and Deep Learning (DL) models. It forecasts mental health plus stress. Two distinct components are developed, with one handling structured numerical and categorical features. Inputs that are demographic and that are lifestyle-related get used in the XGBoost-based component while, inputs is free-text gets processed by the LSTM neural network so as to identify psychological conditions. To classify mental health conditions, the DL model interprets user-submitted textual descriptions of emotional states, along with the ML model using features like gender, occupation, country, treatment history, with behavior patterns to predict a stress mood index. A Flask-based web application deploys both models so users can input data plus they obtain quick helpful feedback. The combined system shows that using multi-modal AI techniques effectively improves mental health diagnostics as well as allowing early intervention.

title: Architectural Patterns and Security: Cloud Based IoT in Smart City Operations
authors: Paul N. Isibor, Karpoor Shashidhar, Amar Rasheed
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: Continuous technological progression has gradually produced an improvement in internet of things (IoT) cyber-physical systems application and creation. There is a need to understand how the sensitive data these devices attain are stored and remain available for accessibility during operation. This study will provide information on how IoT devices take advantage of cloud service providers to store data for availability and integrity over the duration of device operation. Applicability to different scenarios affects how these devices leverage cloud capabilities, which leads to the investigation of different cloud architectures that can be hosted on cloud services.

title: Adaptive Quality of Experience Optimization in Wireless Multimedia Sensor Networks: A Cross-Layer Analytical Approach
authors: Mohammed Adnan Saihood, Hamid Ali Abed Al-Asadi, Farzin Yaghmaee
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: The paper introduces a cross-layer analytical model of the optimization of the Quality of Experience (QoE) in the Wireless Multimedia Sensor Networks (WMSNs). The proposed strategy combines parameters at the physical, network and application layers to enforce dynamic adjustment of the multimedia transmission when conditions in the network change. The model allows real-time regulation of the encoding rate and the routing path by taking into account the joint influence of such factors as the packet loss, delay, jitter, and perceptual video quality to attain increased satisfaction of end-users. The model incorporates a real-time adaptive control mechanism that continuously adjusts video bitrate, routing decisions, and transmission power based on ongoing QoE estimation. Lightweight feedback loops operating across the physical, network, and application layers, enabling the system to react instantly to variations in channel quality, delay, and packet loss, govern these adjustments. The analytical controller applies gradient-based update rules to maintain optimal QoE while respecting energy constraints, ensuring efficient and perceptually aware multimedia delivery. Findings of the simulation show that the adaptive mechanism can greatly enhance the QoE measures with energy efficiency and network stability as compared to the conventional QoS-based schemes. The results indicate that user-centric design is significant in the design of intelligent WMSN systems in multimedia applications.

title: IoT-Enabled Omnidirectional Surveillance Robot with Cloud-Based Face Recognition
authors: Arun Koushik B A, Chakrika Yedluri, K Manasa, Manjunath K, Sheela S
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: The rapid growth of connected devices within domains such as Internet of Things (IoT), embedded computing, and cloud-based AI has altered the terrain of autonomous surveillance technologies considerably. Present-day security systems require high degrees of movement and intelligence to be able to find their way through complicated indoor environments, process visual data on the spot, and react to changing situations without the need for human intervention. In order to fulfill these requirements, this article introduces a comprehensively integrated IoT-enabled omnidirectional surveillance robot featuring cloud-based face recognition through AWS Rekognition. The suggested robot utilizes a holonomic omnidirectional wheel configuration to effect smooth 360° movement, ultrasonic sensors for the robot to locate obstacles in its path without human intervention, and a Raspberry Pi 5 as the onboard microcontroller. The system continuously takes snapshots of the scene through a camera connected to the robot, uploads them to AWS S3, and calls face detection and recognition to be performed by Rekognition. Moving past traditional IoT robotics, this project delivers a physically mobile yet cloud-intelligent solution by seamlessly combining omnidirectional movement, autonomous navigation, scalable AI-based analytics, and real-time cloud connectivity into a unified surveillance platform. The results further establish the feasibility of employing compact cloud-assisted robots for indoor surveillance applications in academic, commercial, and industrial environments.

title: IoT-Vision System for Human Identification and Presence Counting Using Deep Learning
authors: Dhanush S Gowda, Bhuvan Bellad, Bhuvan Madhusudhan, Jaipreet Singh, Sheela S
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: This paper presents an IoT-enabled vision system for real-time human identification and presence counting in smart environments using deep learning. The proposed framework integrates YOLOv8 for person detection, ByteTrack for multi-object tracking, and a MobileNet-based face recognition module within an edge–fog computing architecture. Experimental evaluation demonstrates a YOLOv8 detection performance of 0.9346 mAP@0.5, face recognition accuracy of 98.46%, and stable real-time operation at 18-22 FPS on edge devices, with an end-to-end latency of approximately 120 ms. The system reliably supports identity-aware occupancy analytics, entry-exit monitoring, and real-time alerts, making it suitable for deployment in smart campuses, offices, and public infrastructure.

title: Towards Smart Manufacturing: AI-Driven Prediction and Analysis of Surface Roughness in Machined Components
authors: Shivananda Moolya, Suvarna Kulal, Pallavi Shetty, Faisal Abdullah Al-Kiyumi, Abdullah Saleh Al Farsi, Abdul Aziz Salim Al Nahdi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Surface finish is a critical quality attribute in machining processes, directly influencing the performance of the final product, its functionality, and its aesthetic appearance. Traditional approaches for determining surface roughness rely mainly on experimental measurements and previously derived empirical models, which are time-consuming, less accurate, and more costly. With the adoption of new technologies in industries adopting smart manufacturing, intelligent, data-driven methods are emerging as powerful tools for process optimization in machining. This study explores the application of artificial intelligence (AI) techniques to predict surface finish in machining operations. By controlling process parameters such as cutting speed, feed rate, depth of cut, and tool geometry, AI models are trained to learn complex, nonlinear relationships governing surface quality. The original XGBoost Regressor model was the best-performing model among those tested before tuning, as evidenced by its excellent R-squared score of roughly 0.937. Speed is the most crucial predictor of surface roughness, according to SHAP analysis.

title: Evaluating Fog Simulators for Real-Time IoT Deployment: Towards Smart Parking System
authors: Deepa Arora, Oshin Sharma
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: Fog Computing is a novel technology that connects Internet of Things (IoT) devices to cloud using decentralized computation. It is difficult to evaluate real time case studies in fog computing environment. Therefore, simulations are used for early phase assessments to process and access real time applications in innovative fog criteria. There are multiple simulators but choosing the best simulator for latency sensitive applications is difficult. This study analyzes 17 fog simulators dynamically and evaluates iFogSim, iFogSim2 and MyiFogSim utilizing a smart parking system as an IoT case study. The comparison ensures fairness and reproducibility using similar topology, workloads and resource availability for fairness. Performance is evaluated by latency, network usages and fog resource utilization. Simulator iFogSim 2 outperforms other simulators by lowering latency and communication overhead, enhancing smart parking implementation responsiveness and scalability. The benchmarking method provides simulation selection assistance and a valid evaluation framework for fog-based smart city applications.

title: Toward Schema-Based BI for Cleanroom Experimental Metadata: Organization, Visualization, and a Beta-Stage Case Study

title: Energy-Saving Control of DC Motor Speed via Adaptive Fixed-Time Sliding Mode
authors: Zhiqiang Li, Kun Luo, Liang Tao, Yan Zhou
publish: IEEE Canadian Journal of Electrical and Computer Engineering
abstract: This article presents an adaptive fixed-time sliding mode energy-saving control method for DC motors (DCMs) under parameter uncertainties and load disturbances. A terminal sliding surface is designed to guarantee fixed-time convergence of speed tracking errors independent of initial states. Adaptive laws (ALs) estimate key parameter variations online to enhance robustness. A gradient-descent-based term $u_{energy}=-\uplambda u$ is embedded into the control voltage to minimize copper loss E=∫u2dt. Lyapunov analysis proves global fixed-time stability and derives the sufficient condition $\uplambda <2K/\| u \|_{max}^{2}$. Experiments on a DCM system show that, compared with PID and traditional sliding mode control (SMC), the proposed methods reduce convergence time (CT) by 71.93%, tracking error under load disturbances by 69.87%, energy loss by 15.02%, and improve closed-loop bandwidth by 42.5%. The synergistic design achieves high precision, strong robustness, and low energy consumption, offering an effective solution for high-performance motor control.

title: Can Vibration Patterns Identify Users? Authentication for Smartphone-Watch Collaboration
authors: Zicheng Cui, Zhihai Yang, Zhiquan He, Chenxu Kong, Jianhua He, Jianxin Li, Pinghui Wang, Zhiquan Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: With increasing popularity of mobile smart devices user privacy and security are becoming particularly critical. Traditional authentication methods such as passwords and fingerprints, face the risk of forgery attacks and replay attacks, which are difficult to provide anti-imitation identity discrimination. This paper investigates the propagation characteristics of active vibration signals in hand-wrist coordination during natural touch operations by users, and models their response patterns. We found that there are differences in hand-wrist structure and micro-dynamic behavior among different individuals, which exhibits stable and distinguishable dynamic characteristics in vibration response. Based on the observation, we propose a dual-terminal joint identity authentication system (called VIP) for both smartphone and smartwatch. The system first generates vibration by dual-terminal motors and collects response signals using built in accelerometers, gyroscopes, and magnetometers to extract differential response patterns of users in natural touch. Then, we design a discriminative model, TouchFormer, that can extract direction sensitive features in multi-axis vibrations and achieve dynamic alignment and time compensation at critical moments, effectively integrating asynchronous and cross-device response information. Furthermore, we construct multi-device datasets of thirty users in real-world scenarios to validate the effectiveness of VIP. Extensive experiments demonstrate that VIP performs well on different devices, with an average authentication accuracy improvement of 5.65% compared to existing methods. In addition, the false acceptance rates of VIP under simulated attacks and replay attacks are 1.55% and 2.71%, respectively, which are on average 0.61 and 0.28 percentage points lower than existing methods.

title: MS-SwinNet: Early Fusion of RGB and Multispectral Imagery for Post-Earthquake Building Damage Segmentation
authors: Longkun Zhang, Liqiong Chen, Jianzhong Lu
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Rapid and accurate mapping of earthquake-induced building damage is essential for emergency response and resource allocation, yet post-earthquake urban scenes often contain fragmented structures, severe class imbalance, and heterogeneous backgrounds that make damaged buildings difficult to separate from bare soil, degraded roads, and other confusing materials. This study presents MS-SwinNet, a multi-source semantic segmentation network based on shifted-window transformers that jointly uses three-band RGB imagery and eight-band multispectral imagery for post-earthquake building-damage mapping. The method adopts direct channel-level fusion so that spatial detail and spectral information are learned together from the input stage. Evaluation is conducted on imagery acquired after the 2023 Kahramanmaraş earthquake sequence in Turkey under an operationally realistic small-to-medium data regime with 107 retained geographic units and strict geographic isolation among model-development subsets. Experiments show that direct Early Fusion is more effective than the tested heavier dual-branch design at the present data scale. Compared with a strong RGB-only transformer baseline, the proposed method improves damaged-building recall from 55.21% to 64.72%, IoU from 47.91% to 52.32%, and F1 score from 64.78% to 68.70%. Additional band-removal experiments show that the visible multispectral subset provides the most significant numerical gain, while the red-edge and near- infrared extensions further enhance the spectral characterization of structural debris. These results indicate that multi-source transformer-based segmentation provides a practical high-recall pathway for post-disaster building-damage mapping.

title: Enhancing Path Following of Underactuated Water-Cleaning ASVs Using Adaptive MPC: Real-Time Parameter Estimation and Sensitivity Analysis in Dynamic Marine Environments
authors: Abdelhaleem Saad, Dong Trong Nguyen, Waseem Akram, Shubham Garg, Irfan Hussain
publish: IEEE Journal of Oceanic Engineering
abstract: Autonomous surface vehicles (ASVs) offer a scalable solution for marine debris collection, but their dynamics change as trash accumulates. This work explores adaptive control for underactuated water-cleaning ASVs using model-predictive control (MPC) with real-time parameter estimation via moving horizon estimation (MHE). While MHE effectively tracked linear drag variations, it required sufficient excitation for added mass estimation and was computationally expensive. We also analyzed the sensitivity of explicit hydrodynamic estimation to measurement noise and control inputs, revealing potential inaccuracies. A thorough sensitivity study on nominal MPC under varying hydrodynamic conditions, based on the assumption of simulated trash accumulation effects on system dynamics, motivated the development of an adaptive MPC controller, which showed better performance in extreme scenarios (e.g., heavy trash accumulation and strong currents). Field experiments further indicated that wind-induced currents had a greater impact on MPC performance than trash accumulation. In addition, we developed a comprehensive Python simulation platform to incorporate real-time disturbances, highlighting MPC’s limitations when the model is inaccurate. These findings underscore the need for robust control strategies in dynamic marine environments, paving the way for improved autonomy in water-cleaning ASVs.

title: BrachyUtils: A High-throughput and Modular Brachytherapy Treatment Planning System Built for Python Scripting
authors: Hossein Jafarzadeh, Sébastien Quetin, Jonathan Kalinowski, Farhood Farahnak, Shirin A. Enger
publish: IEEE Transactions on Radiation and Plasma Medical Sciences
abstract: BrachyUtils is a modular Python framework designed to streamline reproducible research in high dose rate (HDR) brachytherapy. It addresses limitations in complex clinical workflows that constrain plan quality under time pressure, where automation tools exist but lack standardized benchmarking and integration. The framework integrates open-source and in-house tools via Docker for reproducibility, connecting key components: registration (OpenTPS, Plastimatch, SimpleElastix), dose calculation/analysis (RapidBrachyTG43, RapidBrachyMC), and optimization (Gurobi, AMPL, ORTools). It also manages data anonymization and file conversions. Benchmarking on the μ-RegPro prostate dataset and 13 in-house HDR prostate plans demonstrated its effectiveness. Contour-based registration outperformed image-based methods; SimpleElastix-BSpline achieved the top prostate Dice score (0.94 ± 0.02) and the lowest Hausdorff distance (4.87 mm ± 1.58 mm) for biopsy contours, while OpenTPS-rigid was fastest (0.89 s ± 0.01 s). Dosimetry comparisons showed that RapidBrachyTG43’s dose-to-water overestimated dose volume histogram (DVH) metrics by 3.3%–6.8% versus RapidBrachyMC’s dose-to-medium. Among solvers, Gurobi excelled with the shortest times (0.59 s ± 0.28 s) for linear/ quadratic penalties. Overall, BrachyUtils unifies data handling, registration, dosimetry, and optimization in a reproducible environment, enabling automated evaluations and accelerating treatment planning research. The rapidly evolving framework can be accessed at https://github.com/engerlab/brachyutils.git.

title: Dielectric Characterization of Hard Dental Tissues at Microwave Frequencies: Experimental Validation of First-Order Debye Behavior
authors: Farzaneh Afkhami, Syed Akbar Raza Naqvi, Ove A. Peters, Amin Abbosh
publish: IEEE Journal of Electromagnetics, RF and Microwaves in Medicine and Biology
abstract: Accurate dielectric characterization of dental hard tissue is a prerequisite for the development and validation of microwave-based dental diagnostic approaches. Existing studies are limited by small sample sizes or reliance on simulated models, resulting in a lack of reliable experimental data on hard dental tissues. This study reports broadband dielectric measurements of hard dental tissues using 14 bovine teeth, which are widely used as an alternative for human teeth in dental research due to their comparable properties. The characterization was performed over the 1–14 GHz range using an open-ended coaxial probe. Careful sample preparation was applied to ensure accuracy and reproducibility. The results show that the dielectric response of hard dental tissues differs from that of water-rich biological tissues due to their mineral-rich and collagen-based composition. Most importantly, the frequency-dependent behavior of these tissues is accurately described by the first-order Debye model. The frequency-dependent dielectric model reported in this study provides a physically consistent baseline for electromagnetic forward simulations, near-field sensor design optimization, and algorithm development for such sensors.

title: MSCAC-TCAM: An Interpretable Fault Diagnosis Method for Rolling Bearings Based on Multisensor Data Fusion
authors: Yongyong Hui, Yali Sun, Gaolei Mao, Xiaoqiang Zhao
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: In industrial systems, timely and accurate fault diagnosis of rolling bearings is critical to ensuring safe and stable operation of equipment. However, in scenarios with varying noise and varying loads, key fault features in bearing vibration signals are difficult to extract sufficiently, resulting in degraded diagnostic performance and insufficient reliability. To address these issues, an interpretable fault diagnosis method for rolling bearings based on multisensor data fusion is designed, referred to as MSCAC-TCAM. First, a multisensor data adaptive fusion module (MSAF) is designed to perform two-stage fusion on raw multisensor signals using dynamic kurtosis weighting and global channel adaptive calibration, highlighting fault-sensitive signals and suppressing noise components. Second, a multiscale cascaded asymmetric convolution module (MSCAC) is constructed to achieve parallel multiscale hierarchical feature extraction and efficient fusion of the fused signal via cascaded asymmetric convolutions. Third, a temporal channel attention mechanism (TCAM) is proposed to adaptively enhance critical fault moments and discriminative channels in fault features in parallel and collaboratively along both temporal and channel dimensions. Finally, experimental validation is carried out on the Case Western Reserve University (CWRU) bearing dataset and the self-constructed PT890 dataset. Interpretability analysis of the multisensor fusion process and the model’s feature learning mechanism is performed using weight visualization, gradient-weighted class activation mapping (Grad-CAM), and quantitative indicators. The results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior diagnostic performance and robustness, and its interpretability improves the reliability of diagnostic results.

title: Pinching-Antenna System Design under Random LoS and NLoS Channels
authors: Yanqing Xu, Yang Lu, Zhiguo Ding, Tsung-Hui Chang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications
abstract: Pinching antennas, realized through position-adjustable radiating elements along dielectric waveguides, have emerged as a promising flexible-antenna technology thanks to their ability to dynamically reshape large-scale channel conditions. However, most existing studies focus on idealized LoS-dominated environments, overlooking the stochastic nature of realistic wireless propagation. This paper investigates a more practical multiuser pinching-antenna system under a composite probabilistic channel model that captures distance-dependent LoS blockage and NLoS scattering. To account for both efficiency and reliability aspects of communication, two complementary design metrics are considered: an average signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) metric characterizing long-term link quality and fairness, and an outage-constrained metric ensuring a prescribed reliability level. Based on these metrics, we formulate two optimization problems: the first seeks to maximize the minimum average SNR across users, while the second seeks to maximize a guaranteed SNR threshold subject to per-user outage constraints. Although both problems are inherently nonconvex, we exploit their underlying monotonic structures and develop low-complexity, bisection-based algorithms that achieve globally optimal solutions using only simple scalar evaluations. Extensive simulations validate the effectiveness of the proposed methods and demonstrate that pinching-antenna systems significantly outperform conventional fixed-antenna designs even under random LoS and NLoS channels.

title: An IRS Assisted Flexible Space Shift Keying Scheme with Pattern Modulation
authors: Qingjiao Xia, Yuyang Peng, Qi Jin, Mohammad Meraj Mirza, Fawaz Al-Hazemi, Juho Lee
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: In this letter, we propose an intelligent reflecting surface (IRS) assisted flexible space shift keying with pattern modulation scheme for high spectral efficient wireless transmission. By designing pattern information into grouped IRS elements and jointly exploiting flexible spatial index modulation, the proposed scheme effectively increases the data rate without requiring additional active radio frequency (RF) chains. A joint maximum likelihood (ML) detector is employed, through which a theoretical bit error rate expression is derived to evaluate the error performance of the proposed scheme. Both theoretical analysis and simulation results demonstrate that the proposed scheme provides flexible configurability and maintains low detection complexity, while achieving reliable performance in high spectral efficient scenarios.

title: Accurate and Robust: Dynamic Image Refinement for Remote Sensing Visual Grounding
authors: Xiaowen Zhang, Lingling Li, Long Sun, Licheng Jiao, Xu Liu, Fang Liu, Wenping Ma, Shuyuan Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Recent advancements in natural image visual grounding have shown promising results. However, existing approaches struggle with precise grounding in remote sensing (RS) images. This challenge arises from two key issues. First, the prevalence of small objects and dense spatial distributions in RS images makes accurate localization difficult. Second, the large domain gap and lack of contextual priors in RS data hinder language-vision alignment. In this paper, we propose RefineVG, a novel framework for fine-grained visual grounding in high-resolution RS images. Unlike methods that require training separate models for coarse localization and fine-grained refinement, RefineVG employs a unified model that iteratively refines predictions. Specifically, RefineVG first predicts a coarse bounding box using a multi-scale cross-modality encoder and a Transformer-based decoder, which progressively align and fuse visual and textual feature. Then, the predicted region is enlarged to capture additional contextual feature. Combined with only category information as text, the cropped region is then re-encoded by the same model to obtain a refined grounding result. Finally, a fusion block combines the coarse and refined predictions. To support this process, we propose a data augmentation strategy by randomly expanding ground truth bounding boxes in training dataset, simulating spatial uncertainty.Extensive experiment results show that RefineVG achieves outstanding performance on three benchmarks, outperforming the second-best method by 5.11% on the DIOR-RSVG dataset, 8.61% on the OPT-RSVG dataset, and 3.61% on the RSVG-HR dataset in P@0.9. Our code are available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/XiaowZhang00/RefineVG</uri>.

title: “The Last Human Will See What We Saw”: The Test of the World's First Atomic Bomb Left its Observers Stupefied
authors: Emily Seyl
publish: IEEE Spectrum
abstract: In the North 10,000 photography bunker, Berlyn Brixner was listening to the countdown on a loudspeaker, his head inside a turret loaded with cameras and film. He was one of the only people instructed to look toward the blast—through his welder's glasses—ready to follow the path of the fire-ball as it launched into the sky. The two Mitchell movie cameras at his station would deliver the best footage to come out of the Trinity test, used by Los Alamos scientists to make some of the first measurements of the effects of a nuclear explosion.

title: Theoretical Model for the Rate-Constrained Multichannel Wiener Filter in Binaural Hearing Aid Applications
authors: Vitor Gelsleicher Probst Curtarelli, Márcio Holsbach Costa
publish: IEEE Transactions on Audio, Speech and Language Processing
abstract: Binaural hearing aids (HAs) are embedded battery powered electronic systems that compensate for bilateral hearing loss. A key advantage of using intercommunicating devices over two independent HAs is their enhanced noise reduction capability, thereby improving speech intelligibility and acoustic comfort. This improvement is achieved by exchanging information between the right and left HAs via a wireless link. This paper proposes a novel theoretical model for predicting the noise-reduction performance of binaural HAs when the wireless communication link is subject to arbitrary bit-rate constraints implemented via quantization operations. It provides a valuable tool for hearing aid designers to understand and configure the parameters necessary to achieve desired performance levels. This is particularly relevant in low-battery scenarios, where bit-rate communication is decreased to avoid premature device shutdown. The model's performance was assessed using objective intelligibility and quality metrics and compared with those obtained from predictions using the widely used Widrow's model, which approximates quantization effects as independent, uniformly distributed additive noise. The modeling errors for both approaches were calculated against results from Monte Carlo simulations. Computational simulations provide strong evidence that the proposed model yields lower intelligibility and quality prediction errors. This allows more accurate modeling of the impact of rate-constrained wireless communication on binaural HAs, and provides more accurate predictions of the trade-off between noise-reduction performance and the quantization of the transmitted information, which affects battery lifetime.

title: Beyond One-Layer Decision: Hierarchical Multi-view Clustering with Progressive Cross-Layer Fusion
authors: Kaixuan Zhou, Pengyuan Li, Jiahui Zhang, Dongxia Chang, Yiming Wang, Yao Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Deep multi-view clustering has demonstrated remarkable efficacy by leveraging the powerful feature extraction capabilities of autoencoders. However, existing autoencoder-based methods typically rely solely on final-layer embeddings of the encoder for downstream tasks, ignoring rich hierarchical information embedded in intermediate layers. To address this, we propose a novel Hierarchical-Aware Multi-view Clustering (HAMVC) framework that integrates complementarity across layers to promote intra-cluster compactness and inter-cluster separability. Specifically, we design a cross-layer affinity propagation module that implements a shallow-to-deep refinement strategy. By leveraging a multi-step diffusion mechanism, this module progressively integrates fine-grained structural patterns from shallow layers into semantic-rich deep features, establishing a robust progressive refinement path. Furthermore, to regulate interactions among heterogeneous views, we introduce a manifold-invariant diversity learning module. This module enforces a decentralized alignment strategy to capture the shared intrinsic manifold across views, while simultaneously imposing a diversity constraint to preserve view-specific complementary information and prevent representation collapse. Extensive experiments on several real-world datasets demonstrate that HAMVC achieves substantial improvements over state-of-the-art methods.

title: An Overview of Microwave Two-Port Measurement Methods for Absorber Materials
authors: Mehmet Faruk Cengiz, Robert Vogt-Ardatjew, Niek Moonen, Frank Leferink
publish: IEEE Letters on Electromagnetic Compatibility Practice and Applications
abstract: Electromagnetic absorber materials are commonly characterized using two-port measurement techniques at microwave frequencies. These techniques directly measure complex S-parameters. Other quantities, such as material properties including relative permittivity and relative permeability, reflectivity, and absorption cross section, are obtained from these measurements. Different measurement methods describe absorber behavior at different levels. The relations between these levels are based on models and assumptions, which are not always accurate. Some measurement methods allow material-level information to be translated into higher level performance metrics through modeling. Other methods directly provide higher level quantities but do not provide detailed material specific information. Understanding these differences is essential for correct interpretation of measurement results. This paper provides an overview of common two-port measurement methods and explains what each method actually measures. Practical aspects such as calibration requirements, sample preparation, usable frequency range, setup complexity, and measurement challenges are briefly discussed. The main advantages and limitations of each measurement method are highlighted to support engineers in selecting the most appropriate approach for their specific application.

title: Diverse-Input Yield Prediction (DIYP) Model Applied Across Different Years with Variable Input Days
authors: Yen-Jen Chang, Ming-Hsin Lai, Yu-Dian Chen, Chien-Ho Wang, Yu-Shun Huang
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The drone-collected multispectral data are useful in agriculture applications, particularly for crop yield prediction. However, the drone flying is often impacted by weather conditions, resulting in data miss or inaccurate information. In this paper, we propose a new rice yield prediction model, named Diverse-Input Yield Prediction (DIYP), which is based on the LSTM network and further enhanced with attention mechanisms and an encoder structure to address the varied input and cross-year prediction challenges. Our model can reduce the impact of missing data, and improve the yield prediction accuracy across different years. All rice datasets are provided by Taiwan Agricultural Research Institute, which consist of multispectral vegetation index data collected by drones. We built our DIYP model using only the 2020 dataset. The experimental results show that the proposed DIYP model outperforms state-of-the-art approaches across multiple metrics, achieving significant performance gains for both the 2021 and 2022 datasets. For the 2021 dataset, the RMSE decreases by 9-15%, while the ACC increases by 9-13%. Similarly, for the 2022 dataset, the RMSE is reduced by 5-23%, and the ACC is improved by 9%. Furthermore, our model achieves Kappa coefficients of 0.83 and 0.84 for 2021 and 2022, respectively, indicating strong agreement with ground truth measurements. In addition to conventional performance metrics, RMSE-based model selection criteria, including AIC, SBIC, HQIC, and AICc, were incorporated to provide a more rigorous comparison among the related models. The information-criterion-based results further confirm that DIYPv3 provides a favorable balance between prediction accuracy and model complexity.

title: A Comparative Study of Attention-Based Transformer and Deep CNN Architectures for Lithography Hotspot Detection in Advanced Process Nodes
authors: Krishnananda Shet, Manjunath Subramanyam, R. Manjunatha, S Ganesh, Mahesh Koti, Kumar N. Krishna Murthy
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: As VLSI technology continuously expands, especially below the 10-nm node, lithography-related process variations have become a major issue in semiconductor manufacturing. Consequently, traditional physics-based verification methods are becoming less practical for full-chip analysis, mainly due to their heavy computational demands and timing constraints. We explore the deep learning techniques for lithography hotspot detection in this work. Two architectures are examined: a Deep Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and a lightweight Vision Transformer (ViT). Both models were trained and tested on the same dataset of layout clips. The input data is represented using a four-channel lithography-aware format that includes both intensity and gradient details to better capture the layout characteristics. The results of the experiment shows that theCNNhas an accuracy of 97.7%. TheVision Transformer gets a lower accuracy than CNN, but needs only 27,809 trainable parameters which makes it considerably more lightweight. Its self-attention mechanism allows it to learn long-range spatial relationships between layout features. CNNs tend to focus on local feature extraction while Transformer-based models are better suited for pulling in broader spatial context from the layout. As advanced process nodes increasingly exhibit non-local printing effects driven by optical proximity and complex process interactions, models capable of capturing distant feature relationships become particularly valuable. The compact size, scalability, and contextual reasoning capability of Transformer-based approaches indicate strong potential for deployment in next-generation Electronic Design Automation workflows focused on Design for Manufacturability optimization.

title: Idle Nodes Detection and Reactivation Aware-Buffer Management in Delay Tolerant Network Using Iemata and Spko
authors: Ajay Prasad, Sandeep Gurung, Kalpana Sharma
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: In Delay Tolerant Networks (DTNs), buffer management is crucial for optimized message delivery by focusing on message storage and prioritization. However, existing studies do not determine the state of a node, leading to buffer space wastage. Therefore, this paper presents a buffer management framework based on Idle Node Detection and Reactivation in DTN. Initially, nodes are initialized in DTN, followed by assessment of Time To Live (TTL) and message expiry detection. Non-expired and new messages are then load-balanced using Kolmogorov-Smirnov Logarithmic Jaynes K-Means (KSLJK-Means). Subsequently, the load-balanced messages are signed with the Digital Logarithmic Double Hashing Probing Signature Algorithm (DLDHPSA). Before message transmission, the idle/active state of nodes is identified using the Idle Exponential Moving Average Timer Algorithm (IEMATA). If a node is idle, it is activated via periodic beaconing. Next, messages are prioritized using Lorentzian Raised Cosine Fuzzy (LRC-Fuzzy). Afterward, messages are allocated to buffers following successful digital signature verification. Buffer utilization rates are estimated to determine available space; if space is available, messages are stored in the buffer. If the buffer is full, messages are dropped according to the Message Utility Score (MUS) based drop policy. The Shubert Pied Kingfisher Optimizer (SPKO) is employed for buffer allocation. The proposed model achieves a low buffer allocation time of 2165 ms. It offers significant benefits when deployed in satellite communications, vehicular networks, and IoT systems. Overall, the model provides resource-centric solutions to enhance the reliability of DTN environments.

title: PROMEOS: Protein-protein interaction prediction using a Mixture of Experts and multimodal learning by integrating Ontology and Sequences
authors: Jae-Woo Chu, Jong-Hoon Park, Gwang-Hyeon Yun, Young-Rae Cho
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Protein–protein interactions (PPIs) are fundamental to numerous biological processes, yet their experimental identification remains costly and time-consuming. Deep learning has emerged as a promising approach for PPI prediction, but many existing methods struggle to achieve high accuracy due to insufficiently leveraging complementary modalities. In addition, they face limitations in generalizing beyond specific experimental datasets and encounter challenges in training due to the large scale of PPI data. To address these challenges, we propose PROMEOS, PPI prediction using a mixture of experts and multimodal learning by integrating ontology and sequences. Across diverse experimental settings, PROMEOS outperformed strong baselines, achieving relative gains of 2.4% on the yeast dataset and 2.02% on the human dataset over the second-best model. Moreover, it achieved a 6.94% improvement in cross-species prediction and a 7.9% increase in prediction accuracy during confidence-threshold transfer evaluation. While more than doubling the parameter count with sparse mixture of experts, inference time increased by only 3.5%, and removing the decoder reduced training time approximately 10%. Overall, PROMEOS provides an effective solution for PPI prediction in terms of accuracy, applicability, and efficiency.

title: EEG-Based ADHD Classification Using Machine Learning and Boosting Algorithms
authors: Debasish Phukan, Daisy Das, Nabamita Deb
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) is a common neurodevelopmental disorder, and EEG-based biomarkers may help support clinical diagnosis. In this study, EEG data from 61 children with ADHD and 60 healthy controls (ages 7–12) were analyzed using multiple machine learning models. The signals were preprocessed using bandpass filtering (1–40 Hz), 50 Hz notch filtering, standardization, and ICA. Features were extracted using spectral and nonlinear methods, including band power, Katz fractal dimension, detrended fluctuation analysis, and Lempel–Ziv complexity. Twelve classifiers were evaluated using an 80:20 stratified train–test split, where 80% of the data were used for training and 20% for testing and performance was measured using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC. Among all methods, boosting models performed best, with XGBoost achieving the highest accuracy (92.81%), followed by Gradient Boosting (92.69%). KNN (90.30%) was the strongest traditional model, while Gaussian Naïve Bayes showed the lowest performance (51.02%), likely due to its simplifying feature-independence assumption. Overall, boosting models showed strong potential, but testing on larger and more diverse datasets is still needed.

title: Applications and Limitations of Large Language Models in Digital Forensics: A Survey
authors: Skyler Ashton Saunders, Mohamed I. Ibrahem, Mahmoud Abouyoussef
publish: 2026 IEEE 2nd International Conference on Secure IoT, Assured and Trusted Computing (SATC)
abstract: Digital forensics (DF) plays a critical role in investigating and analyzing digital evidence across criminal and civil cases. Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) have introduced new opportunities to support forensic workflows by automating or assisting tasks such as dataset generation, evidence extraction, analysis, reporting, and investigative support. Despite growing interest in this area, existing studies remain fragmented and lack a comprehensive examination of how LLMs are applied in digital forensics, as well as their associated limitations. This survey systematically reviews recent research on the use of LLMs in digital forensics, categorizing current applications and summarizing representative techniques and outcomes. In addition, the paper analyzes key challenges, including hallucinations, data privacy, explainability, performance cost, and legal admissibility, that currently limit real-world adoption. Finally, the survey outlines open research problems and future directions necessary to enable trustworthy and effective integration of LLMs into digital forensic investigations.

title: Chennamane: The Historiography, Gameplay, and Cultural Revival of an Obscure Folk Game from Southern India
authors: Gurunandan G R, Devika Udupa V, Bharath S Bhat, Prasanna Kumar, Clion Franstin Miranda, Gururaj K
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: A classic board game from South India, chennemane also known as Mancala game, is frequently played with cowrie shells and wooden boards. The goal of this project is to use the game engine known as Unity to convey the cultural significance and strategic depth of Chennemane towards the realm of the web. Our goal in digitising this beloved game is to uphold its legacy.While enabling a worldwide audience to look at it. Creating an intuitive user experience with intricate visuals and animations while maintaining the genuine feel of an actual physical game is a key component of the game creation process. We employ gaming mechanisms that adhere closely to conventional guidelines, guaranteeing a genuine gameplay experience. The game will also allow players to play with others on the precise same device in both local person vs. player and single-player modes against AI.

title: Hair Baldness Progression Prediction Using XGBoost: Risk Forecasting Model
authors: Mark Maben, Arhath Kumar, Balachandra Rao
publish: 2026 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Data Engineering (AIDE)
abstract: Hair-loss prediction is increasingly important, as early thinning is strongly influenced by genetic, hormonal, and lifestyle factors. This study presents a machine-learning framework for forecasting hair baldness progression using a structured dataset composed of clinical and behavioral indicators such as age, stress levels, hormonal influence, and family history. Data preprocessing included normalization, missing-value imputation, and categorical encoding. An XGBoost classifier was trained to estimate individual hair-loss risk and evaluated using multiple performance metrics, including accuracy, precision, recall, ROC–AUC, and Brier score. The proposed model achieved strong predictive performance, with an accuracy of 90.12 percent and a ROC–AUC of 96.63 percent. Feature-importance analysis and SHAP-based explainability were employed to interpret model decisions, while a five-year risk projection module was developed to estimate future hair-loss progression. The results demonstrate that machine-learning techniques can provide scalable, interpretable, and preventive solutions for early hair-loss risk assessment.
